name: Translation — quantifiers (мало / немного / few / a few)

_meta.rule: мало + uncountable → little; мало + countable → few; немного + uncountable → a little; немного + countable → a few
_meta.answerDistribution: 10 little, 10 few, 5 a little, 5 a few

tasks.stem: We have (мало) ___ time before the meeting, so let's stop chatting and get started.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' carries a negative meaning ('not enough'). With uncountable nouns it translates as 'little'. 'Few' is wrong because 'time' is uncountable. 'A little' would mean 'some', losing the negative sense of 'мало'.

tasks.stem: There are (мало) ___ buses on this route after midnight, so plan your journey carefully.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural noun translates as 'few' (= not many, with negative emphasis). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would lose the negative meaning of 'мало'.

tasks.stem: Could you add (немного) ___ milk to my coffee? I prefer it not too strong.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' carries a positive meaning ('some, not none'). With uncountable nouns it translates as 'a little'. 'A few' is for countable plural nouns. 'Little' would carry a negative meaning ('not enough'), changing the request.

tasks.stem: I bought (немного) ___ apples at the market on my way home from work.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' with a countable plural noun translates as 'a few' (= some, with positive emphasis). 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Few' would carry a negative meaning ('not many').

tasks.stem: She has (мало) ___ patience for small talk, especially after a long day at the clinic.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Patience' is uncountable. 'Мало' (negative) + uncountable noun → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would mean 'some', losing the negative emphasis.

tasks.stem: We invited (мало) ___ guests to the wedding because we wanted a small, intimate ceremony.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural translates as 'few' (= not many). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would mean 'some', which contradicts 'мало'.

tasks.stem: Even at the height of his fame, Charlie Chaplin gave (мало) ___ interviews about his private life.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural noun translates as 'few' (= not many, with negative emphasis). 'Little' is reserved for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would change the meaning to 'some'.

tasks.stem: Albert Einstein had (мало) ___ patience for academic ceremony and often skipped formal dinners.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Patience' is an uncountable abstract noun. 'Мало' + uncountable noun → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would suggest 'some patience', losing the negative sense.

tasks.stem: Vincent van Gogh sold (мало) ___ paintings during his lifetime and only became famous after his death.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural noun translates as 'few' (= not many). The negative emphasis is essential here — Van Gogh did not sell many paintings. 'Little' is for uncountable nouns; 'a few' would weaken the meaning.

tasks.stem: Frida Kahlo received (немного) ___ formal art training before she began painting her famous self-portraits.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Training' is an uncountable noun. 'Немного' + uncountable → 'a little' (= some, with positive emphasis). 'A few' would require a countable plural. 'Little' would mean 'not enough', which misrepresents the historical fact.

tasks.stem: Marie Curie wrote (немного) ___ letters to her daughters every week, even during her busiest research periods.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' with a countable plural noun translates as 'a few' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Few' would mean 'not many', losing the positive sense.

tasks.stem: Throughout his career Stephen Hawking had (мало) ___ time for self-pity, even as his illness progressed.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Time' is uncountable. 'Мало' + uncountable noun → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would mean 'some', softening the negative emphasis.

tasks.stem: After the fall of Constantinople in 1453, (мало) ___ scholars remained in the city to teach Greek.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural noun translates as 'few' (= not many, with negative emphasis). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would change the meaning to 'some', losing the historical implication.

tasks.stem: During the Great Depression there was (мало) ___ work available, and queues outside soup kitchens grew longer every week.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Work' (in the sense of 'employment') is uncountable. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would weaken the sense of severe scarcity.

tasks.stem: After the 1812 fire, (мало) ___ buildings of Moscow's old city centre survived intact.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural noun translates as 'few' (= not many). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would weaken the meaning.

tasks.stem: Medieval monks copied (немного) ___ pages each day, working slowly to keep their handwriting consistent.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' with a countable plural translates as 'a few' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Few' would mean 'not many', losing the positive sense.

tasks.stem: The Roman senators had (мало) ___ influence over Caesar's military decisions during his Gallic campaigns.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Influence' is uncountable in this sense. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would soften the negative emphasis.

tasks.stem: Among the first explorers of Antarctica, (мало) ___ men returned without injuries from the long expedition.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural translates as 'few' (= not many, negative emphasis). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would mean 'some', losing the gravity of the historical situation.

tasks.stem: The early universe contained (мало) ___ heavy elements; most were forged later in the cores of dying stars.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Heavy elements' is a countable plural. 'Мало' + countable → 'few' (= not many). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would not convey the scientific point about scarcity.

tasks.stem: Until the 19th century there was (мало) ___ evidence for the existence of atoms beyond a few philosophical arguments.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Evidence' is uncountable in English. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= not enough). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would soften the negative emphasis.

tasks.stem: Researchers added (немного) ___ sodium to the solution and observed an immediate change in colour.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Sodium' as a substance is uncountable. 'Немного' + uncountable → 'a little' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A few' would require a countable plural. 'Little' would mean 'not enough', changing the experimental description.

tasks.stem: After centuries of debate, scientists today have (мало) ___ doubt that human activity is warming the climate.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Doubt' is uncountable in this idiomatic sense ('have little doubt'). 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= barely any). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would mean 'some doubt', changing the meaning.

tasks.stem: The biologist took (немного) ___ samples from each pond and stored them in numbered glass tubes.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' with a countable plural translates as 'a few' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A little' is for uncountable nouns. 'Few' would mean 'not many', losing the positive sense.

tasks.stem: Modern surgery has (мало) ___ in common with the bloody procedures performed before anaesthesia was discovered.
tasks.explanation: 'Common' here pairs with the uncountable idiom 'have little in common'; 'мало' + uncountable → 'little'.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Have little in common' is a fixed idiom with the uncountable phrase 'in common'. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little'. 'Few' would be ungrammatical here, and 'a little' would soften the contrast intended by the sentence.

tasks.stem: There is (мало) ___ wisdom in promising what you cannot deliver, however good your intentions may be.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Wisdom' is an uncountable abstract noun. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= barely any). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would weaken the moral force of the saying.
tasks.author: Aesop

tasks.stem: (мало) ___ men know how brave they are until courage is the only choice left to them.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural translates as 'few' (= not many). The aphorism makes a negative claim about how many men know themselves. 'Little' is for uncountable nouns; 'a few' would neutralise the rhetorical force.
tasks.author: Marcus Aurelius

tasks.stem: Add (немного) ___ kindness to every conversation, and the world will already be a slightly better place.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Kindness' is an uncountable abstract noun. 'Немного' + uncountable → 'a little' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A few' is for countable plurals. 'Little' would mean 'not enough', contradicting the message.
tasks.source: Adapted · attributed to the Dalai Lama
tasks.author: Dalai Lama

tasks.stem: (немного) ___ hours of honest work each morning will outpace whole days of distracted effort.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Немного' with a countable plural translates as 'a few' (= some, positive emphasis). 'A little' would require an uncountable noun. 'Few' would carry a negative meaning, undermining the productive idea of the saying.
tasks.author: Benjamin Franklin

tasks.stem: There is (мало) ___ joy in winning if you have to betray a friend to do it.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Joy' is an uncountable abstract noun. 'Мало' + uncountable → 'little' (= barely any). 'Few' is for countable plurals. 'A little' would dilute the moral claim.
tasks.author: Sophocles

tasks.stem: (мало) ___ books change a life as much as the ones we re-read in moments of doubt.
tasks.fullExplanation: 'Мало' with a countable plural translates as 'few' (= not many, negative emphasis). 'Little' is for uncountable nouns. 'A few' would mean 'some', losing the rhetorical force of the aphorism.
tasks.author: Italo Calvino
